review_summary: The segment clearly defines the CNN pattern and places it in historical context, so transcript-only grounding is sufficient.

question_bank.question: What is the usual high-level structure of a convolutional neural network for image classification?
choices: A feature-extracting body of convolution, pooling, and nonlinear layers followed by prediction layers.
A single nearest-neighbor lookup table followed by a distance metric.
A hand-written color histogram with no learned parameters.
A recurrent-only model that processes one pixel channel at a time.
question_bank.explanation: CNNs typically use convolutional blocks to build image features and then use prediction layers to turn those features into class scores.

question_bank.question: Why was AlexNet a turning point for computer vision?
choices: It scaled CNN-style models using large data and GPU compute, making deep learning highly effective for vision.
It proved convolution was unnecessary for image tasks.
It replaced training data with manually coded rules.
It was the first model to use color histograms for classification.
question_bank.explanation: AlexNet showed that CNNs could become very powerful when combined with enough data, compute, and end-to-end training.

question_bank.question: Why are CNNs still useful to study even when transformers outperform them on many vision tasks?
choices: They provide important intuition about images and still appear in historical and hybrid vision systems.
They are the only architecture that can process images at all.
They always outperform transformers on every modern benchmark.
They avoid learned representations and therefore need less data.
question_bank.explanation: CNNs encode image-specific ideas such as locality and spatial structure. Those ideas remain valuable for understanding vision models and architectures that mix multiple design patterns.
